entries.definition:
كتاب العين

عين

(عبد) العين والباء والدال أصلانِ صحيحان، كأنَّهما متضادَّان، و[الأول] من ذينك الأصلينِ يدلُّ على لِين وذُلّ، والآخر على شِدّة وغِلَظ.

فالأوّل العَبد، وهو المملوك، والجماعةُ العبيدُ، وثلاثةُ أعبدٍ وهم العِبادُ. قال الخليل: إلاّ أنّ العامة اجتمعوا على تفرقةِ ما بين عباد الله والعبيدِ المملوكين. يقال هذا عبدٌ بيِّن العُبُودَة. ولم نسمَعْهم يشتقُّون منه فعلاً، ولو اشتق لقيل عَبُد، أي صار عبداً وأقرَّ بالعُبُودة، ولكنّه أُمِيت الفعلُ فلم يُستعمل. قال: وأمّا عَبَدَ
entries.definition_english:
### Core Semantic Principles
The letters *Ayn*, *Ba*, and *Dal* (ع ب د) signify two primary, distinct principles within Arabic lexicography. Intriguingly, these principles appear to be antithetical to one another.
*   The **first principle** denotes qualities of **softness (*līn*) and submission/humility (*dhull*)**.
*   The **second principle**, conversely, signifies **severity (*shidda*) and coarseness/roughness (*ghilaẓ*)**.

### Derivations & Evidence
Ibn Faris proceeds to elaborate on the first principle, characterized by submission and humility:

*   The primary exemplar of this principle is the noun **_al-'abd_** (عَبْد), which specifically refers to **the enslaved or owned individual (*al-mamlūk*)**.
*   Ibn Faris notes the plural forms for _al-'abd_:
    *   **_al-'abīd_** (العَبِيد) for a general group of enslaved persons.
    *   **_a'bud_** (أَعبُد) specifically for a group of three enslaved individuals.
    *   He also mentions **_al-'ibād_** (العِبَاد), which refers to servants or worshippers, typically of God. In this context, Ibn Faris cites Al-Khalil (likely Al-Khalil ibn Ahmad al-Farahidi), who noted that common linguistic usage has established a clear differentiation between **_’ibād Allāh_** (servants of God) and **_al-'abīd al-mamlūkīn_** (human-owned enslaved persons).
*   An illustrative phrase for the noun _al-'abd_ is **"This is an _'abd_ whose _al-'ubūda_ (العُبُودَة)"** – meaning, his state of servitude or enslavement – "is evident." This highlights the clear condition of being a slave.
*   Regarding verb derivation from this aspect of the root, Ibn Faris makes a notable linguistic observation: he states that he has not encountered a verb actively used to mean "to become a slave." He postulates that if such a verb were to exist and be properly derived, it would likely be **_’abuda_** (عَبُد, with a *ḍamma* on the *bā’*), signifying "he became a slave" or "he acknowledged servitude/enslavement." However, he concludes that this specific verbal form has become "dead" (*umīta al-fi'l*) in the language, meaning it fell into disuse and is no longer employed.
*   Ibn Faris then transitions to introduce another verbal form, **_’abada_** (عَبَدَ, with a *fatḥa* on the *bā’*), the detailed discussion of which would presumably continue the exploration of this first principle related to submission and humility.

entries.definition:
c-b-d slave, servant, to enslave; obedience, submission, to worship, to adore; to tan camel hide, to tar a boat. Some scholars, apparently with no evidence save for the assumption that spiritual concepts are foreign to Arabic per se, attribute the concept of
594

c-b-d slave, servant, to enslave; obedience, submission, to worship, to adore; to tan camel hide, to tar a boat. Some scholars, apparently with no evidence save for the assumption that spiritual concepts are foreign to Arabic per se, attribute the concept of

entries.definition:
worshipping associated with some derivatives of this root to a borrowing from other Semitic languages. ʾal-Suyūṭī quotes ʾabū ʾal-Qāsim’s suggestion that the sense of ‘to enslave’ is a borrowing from Nabataean. Of this root, 11 forms occur 275 times in the Qurʾan: عبد cabada 121 times; يعبدون yucbadūn once; عبد cabbada once; عبد cabd 28 times; عبدين cabdayn once; عباد cibād 97 times; عبيد cabīd five times; عابد cābid once; عابدات cābidāt once; عابدين cābidūn 10 and عبادة cibādatun nine times.
عبد cabada u [v. trans.] 1 to worship (20:14) لا إله إلا أنا فاعبدني there is no god but Me, so worship Me 2 to serve, to carry out one’s religious duties (53:62) فاسجدوا لله واعبدوا bow down before God and serve (also, worship) [Him].

عبّد cabbada [v. II trans.] to enslave (26:22) وعبدت بني إسرائيل you have enslaved the children of Israel.

عبد cabd I [sing. n.] 1 (in relation to God) a) someone, a person (43:59) إن هو إلا عبد أنعمنا عليه وجعلناه مثلا لبني إسرائيل he is only someone We favoured and made an example for the Children of Israel b) worshipper, servant (19:30) إني عبد الله I am a servant of God 2 anyone (2:221) ولعبد مؤمن خير من مشرك ولو أعجبكم a believing male is better than a male polytheist, even though he may please you 3 slave (16:75) ضرب الله مثلا عبدا مملوكا لا يقدر على شيء and God gives the parable of an owned slave, who has no power over anything 4 (in contrast to أمة) male slave (2:178) الحر بالحر والعبد بالعبد freeman for freeman, slave for slave II [pl. عبيد cabīd] all creatures (50:29) وما أنا بظلام للعبيد I am not unjust to any creature III [pl. عباد cibād] 1 created beings, mortals (7:194) إن الذين تدعون من دون الله عباد أمثالكم those [whom] you [idolaters] call upon instead of God are created things like you 2 all the creation, everything there is, all (2:207) والله رؤوف بالعباد and God is most compassionate to all 3 all living beings (50:11) رزقا للعباد as a provision for all [who may eat] 4 servants of God, worshippers (15:42) إن عبادي ليس لك عليهم سلطان as for My servants, you will have no power over [any of them]; *(25:63) عباد الرحمن the chosen ones [of/by] God, the true believers, the select [lit. the servants of the Beneficent] 5 male slaves (only in 24:32) وأنكحوا الأيامى منكم والصالحين من عبادكم وإمائكم and marry off the single among you and those of

595
